intro: October 2008. The global financial system is melting down. Tesla has one product — a Roadster that's massively over budget — and roughly weeks of cash left. You just took over as CEO. Every automaker in America is begging Washington for a bailout.

realOutcome.summary: Tesla went from near-bankruptcy to the most valuable automaker in the world. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: The Roadster is bleeding money, the credit markets are frozen, and payroll is close. Outside money is nearly impossible to raise. You do, however, still have your own fortune.

choices.text: Put in your own last dollars to keep it alive.
choices.reveal: Musk poured nearly his entire remaining fortune into Tesla in 2008, reportedly borrowing money for rent. It kept the company breathing until outside capital could close.

choices.text: Give up and sell the husk to a big automaker.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A Detroit giant buys the technology, shelves the EV program to protect its gas engines, and the electric car stays a rich hobbyist's toy for another decade.

choices.text: Lay off the engineers and slow the Roadster down.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You conserve cash by gutting the only team that can finish the car. A slower Roadster misses its window and the death spiral just takes longer.

decisions.scenario: There's a financing round on the table, but the terms are ugly and the clock is running out before the holidays. Investors know exactly how desperate you are.

choices.text: Close the round on Christmas Eve, whatever the terms.
choices.reveal: Tesla closed a critical funding round on December 24, 2008 — hours from insolvency. Survival first; you can fix the cap table once you're alive.

choices.text: Hold out for a cleaner deal at a better valuation.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: There is no better deal in December 2008. You run out of cash over the holidays and the company is liquidated in January.

choices.text: Take a lowball buyout offer to end the stress.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You sell for a fraction of the eventual value to escape the fear. The acquirer never ships the Model S.

decisions.scenario: Two lifelines appear: a $465M low-interest loan from the Department of Energy, and the option to take Tesla public — the first American car company IPO since Ford in 1956.

choices.text: Take the DOE loan and IPO the company.
choices.reveal: Tesla took the $465M DOE loan and went public in June 2010. It later repaid the loan nine years early — the public markets and cheap capital funded the Model S.

choices.text: Stay private and grow slowly on revenue.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Without the capital injection you can't tool a real factory. Tesla stays a boutique sports-car shop and never reaches the mass market.

choices.text: Sell a big equity stake to an oil major.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Your new owner has every incentive to slow-walk the electric transition. The mission dies inside a board room.

decisions.scenario: For your next car you can build a clean-sheet luxury sedan — the Model S — or play it safe with a cheap, high-volume econobox. The sedan could bankrupt you; it could also prove electric cars can be desirable.

choices.text: Bet the company on the from-scratch Model S.
choices.reveal: The Model S shipped in 2012 and won Motor Trend Car of the Year. It proved an EV could out-drive and out-desire gas luxury cars, and it made Tesla credible.

choices.text: Build a cheap econobox to move volume first.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A forgettable cheap EV competes on price with everyone and desire with no one. Tesla never earns the premium brand that funds everything later.

choices.text: License your powertrains and stop making cars.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You become a quiet parts supplier to automakers who bury your tech in cars they'd rather not sell. The brand never exists.

decisions.scenario: You have a technology lead but a charging problem: no one buys an electric car they can't road-trip. You can build a proprietary charging network, and you're even considering giving your patents away.

choices.text: Open the patents, build Superchargers and a Gigafactory.
choices.reveal: Tesla open-sourced its patents in 2014 and built out Superchargers and the Gigafactory. Growing the whole EV market — and owning its charging and battery supply — was worth more than hoarding IP.

choices.text: Hoard the patents and sue anyone who copies you.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You defend a small EV market instead of expanding a huge one. Legal fees rise, the category stays niche, and your cars have nowhere to charge.

choices.text: Stay a niche luxury brand and skip the buildout.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Without charging infrastructure and battery scale, Tesla is a cool toy for a few thousand rich early adopters. The mass market never arrives.

bands.line: You went from weeks-from-dead to defining an industry. Legend episode: WON.
Survived, but as a boutique. The mass-market revolution happened to someone else.
Liquidated in the crash. The EV era waits another decade. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Tesla 100x'd after this fork. Today's agents flagged a company everyone left for dead →
